items.q: What is the difference between top-down and bottom-up offender profiling?
items.correct: Top-down fits offenders to pre-set categories (organised/disorganised); bottom-up builds a profile from statistical analysis of the evidence.
items.misconception: They are two names for the same profiling method.
items.random_wrong: Top-down profiling is done by senior officers and bottom-up by junior ones.
items.why: Students confuse the American typology approach with the British data-driven approach.

items.q: Did Lombroso's 'atavistic form' theory have strong scientific support?
items.correct: No; the idea that criminals show primitive physical features is now discredited and lacked proper controls.
items.misconception: It is a well-supported, accurate explanation of crime.
items.random_wrong: Atavistic form refers to a type of prison sentence.
items.why: Students take the historical theory at face value, unaware it is largely rejected.

items.q: Does custodial sentencing reliably reduce reoffending?
items.correct: Not reliably; recidivism rates are often high and prison can have negative as well as deterrent effects.
items.misconception: Prison always successfully stops people reoffending.
items.random_wrong: Custodial sentencing means a fine paid to the court.
items.why: Students assume imprisonment guarantees rehabilitation, ignoring high reoffending rates.

items.q: Top-down offender profiling was developed by:
items.why: Correct answer: FBI. “Canter” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Differential association theory was proposed by:
items.why: Correct answer: Sutherland. “Eysenck” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Lombroso publish his atavistic form theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1876 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
